skill.name: ms365-tenant-manager
skill.description: Plan Microsoft 365 tenant setup, user lifecycle tasks, security checks, DNS records, and administrative PowerShell scripts.
skill.summary: Generate Microsoft 365 tenant administration plans, checklists, and PowerShell automation drafts.
skill.author: alirezarezvani

security_audit.summary: The skill appears intended for legitimate Microsoft 365 administration and no prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found. However, it generates privileged PowerShell that performs tenant-wide changes, and several user-controlled values are inserted into double-quoted PowerShell strings without escaping. Static weak-crypto and env-access alerts were dismissed as false positives, while hardcoded Microsoft URLs are low risk.

high_findings.title: Unescaped Input in Privileged PowerShell Generation
high_findings.description: Tenant, user, policy, and license values are inserted into double-quoted PowerShell strings without escaping or validation. If untrusted input contains quotes, newlines, or PowerShell subexpressions, the generated script could be altered before an administrator runs it.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static command findings are confirmed in semantic context: generated PowerShell embeds input directly into executable script text. The impact is high because the scripts are designed for administrator execution.

high_findings.title: Tenant-Wide Administrative Operations Generated by Default
high_findings.description: Generated scripts request broad Microsoft Graph, Exchange Online, and Teams permissions and perform tenant-changing operations. This is aligned with the skill purpose, but publication should warn users because mistakes can affect users, licenses, audit settings, and mailbox state across a tenant.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly generate commands with write scopes and tenant-changing cmdlets. This is probably legitimate, but it is a confirmed high-impact capability.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Offboarding Script Lacks Built-In Dry Run
medium_findings.description: The offboarding script disables sign-in, revokes sessions, removes group memberships and devices, converts mailboxes, and removes licenses. The documentation recommends dry runs, but this generated offboarding flow does not include a WhatIf or confirmation mode in the shown implementation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destructive operations are explicit, and no dry-run guard appears in the relevant offboarding lines. Operational impact is clear even though the intent is normal administration.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Tenant Inventory Written to Local Reports
medium_findings.description: The generated security audit writes MFA status, admin role assignments, inactive users, guest users, license data, mailbox delegations, and Conditional Access information to CSV files. No network exfiltration was found, but these files can expose sensitive tenant posture if stored or shared carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The CSV export paths and report contents are directly visible in the generated script. The risk is local data exposure rather than confirmed exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Microsoft Service URLs
low_findings.description: The network findings point to Microsoft administration, Graph Explorer, PowerShell Gallery, roadmap, and SharePoint admin URLs. These are expected references for this skill and no evidence of suspicious external collection endpoints was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and all are Microsoft-related service or documentation endpoints. This supports dismissing the static network alerts as low risk.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto and Env-Access Alerts Dismissed
low_findings.description: Weak-crypto alerts correspond to Microsoft 365 security terms such as MFA, passwords, and authentication guidance, not cryptographic code. Env-access alerts correspond to configuration dictionaries, not process environment or secret reads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no hashing, encryption implementation, or environment-secret access at these locations. The terms are documentation or generated admin-script content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic PowerShell Script Assembly
dangerous_patterns.description: Python f-strings assemble executable PowerShell with embedded tenant and user input. Escaping should be added before generated scripts are published or run.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly constructs PowerShell scripts from template strings. The lack of an escaping helper is visible in the reviewed generation paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Microsoft 365 Write Scopes
dangerous_patterns.description: Generated scripts request write permissions such as Directory.ReadWrite.All, User.ReadWrite.All, Group.ReadWrite.All, and policy write scopes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The permission scopes are directly present in the generated PowerShell. They are expected for administration but dangerous if used without review.

content.value_statement: Microsoft 365 tenant administration requires careful planning, repeatable scripts, and security review. This skill creates setup plans, checklists, and PowerShell drafts for tenant, user, license, and security workflows.
actual_capabilities: Creates phased Microsoft 365 tenant setup checklists with priorities and estimated effort.
Generates DNS record guidance for Exchange Online, Teams, device registration, and domain verification.
Drafts PowerShell scripts for tenant setup, Conditional Access, security audits, and bulk license assignment.
Drafts user provisioning and offboarding scripts for Microsoft Graph and Exchange Online workflows.
Recommends licenses and group memberships from user role, department, location, and compliance context.
Validates basic user data fields before provisioning workflows are planned.
limitations: It drafts scripts and guidance; it does not safely execute changes inside Microsoft 365 by itself.
Generated scripts require manual review, tenant-specific testing, and appropriate administrator roles.
Some generated PowerShell inserts input without escaping, so untrusted values must be sanitized first.
Hybrid Active Directory and advanced compliance deployments need additional expert validation.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Tenant Rollout
use_cases.description: Create a phased setup checklist, DNS plan, security baseline, license estimate, and next-step list for a new tenant.

use_cases.title: Standardize User Lifecycle Workflows
use_cases.description: Draft onboarding, license assignment, group membership, and offboarding procedures for repeatable identity operations.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Security Review
use_cases.description: Generate audit script drafts and review areas for MFA, admin roles, inactive accounts, guests, licenses, and mailbox access.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Tenant Setup Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Microsoft 365 tenant setup checklist for a 50-person company using one custom domain and baseline security controls.

prompt_templates.title: Draft User Provisioning Steps
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a user provisioning workflow for new finance employees, including required fields, licenses, groups, and validation checks.

prompt_templates.title: Review Security Audit Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a Microsoft 365 security audit plan covering MFA, admin roles, inactive users, guest users, licenses, and mailbox delegations.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Reviewed PowerShell Drafts
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate PowerShell drafts for bulk license assignment and Conditional Access setup. Include safety checks, review notes, and testing steps before production use.

output_examples.input: Plan a Microsoft 365 tenant setup for a 75-user technology company with GDPR requirements.
output: A phased setup checklist with tenant, domain, security, service, and compliance tasks.
A DNS record summary for Exchange Online, Teams federation, DKIM, and device enrollment.
License distribution recommendations with monthly and annual cost estimates.

output_examples.input: Prepare a security audit for MFA, inactive users, admin roles, and guest access.
output: A PowerShell audit draft that connects to Microsoft Graph and Exchange Online.
A report plan covering MFA status, admin assignments, inactive users, guest accounts, licenses, mailbox delegations, and Conditional Access.
A short remediation checklist for common findings.

output_examples.input: Create an offboarding process for a departing employee.
output: A secure offboarding sequence for disabling sign-in, revoking sessions, removing access, converting the mailbox, and documenting actions.
Warnings to test the script, confirm mailbox handling, and review licenses before production execution.

best_practices: Review every generated PowerShell script line by line before running it with administrator permissions.
Test tenant changes in a non-production tenant or report-only mode before production enforcement.
Use least-privilege roles, managed identities, or approved credential stores for administrative automation.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated scripts directly from untrusted user input or pasted CSV data.
Do not use Global Administrator access when a narrower Microsoft 365 role is sufficient.
Do not store generated audit reports in shared folders without access controls.

faq.question: Does this skill connect to my Microsoft 365 tenant?
faq.answer: No. It generates plans and script drafts. You review and run scripts separately with your own credentials.

faq.question: What permissions are usually required?
faq.answer: Many workflows need Global Administrator or specific Microsoft 365 admin roles. Use the narrowest role that can complete the task.

faq.question: Can I use the generated PowerShell in production?
faq.answer: Only after review, input sanitization, and testing. Several scripts can make tenant-wide changes.

faq.question: Does it support license planning?
faq.answer: Yes. It can recommend license mixes based on user count, role, department, compliance needs, and estimated costs.

faq.question: Does it cover hybrid Active Directory?
faq.answer: Only at a high level. Hybrid identity and AD Connect designs require additional tenant-specific review.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level high?
faq.answer: The skill generates privileged PowerShell and embeds input into scripts. That creates high operational and injection risk without careful review.
